question: At the baptism, why do all three Synoptics stack Father-voice + Son + Spirit in one scene, and how does that shape a clean reply to ‘Trinity is later invention’ polemics?

choices.A: It presents Father, Son, and Spirit together in an early narrative moment, giving a scriptural triadic pattern Christians later articulate as Trinitarian doctrine.
choices.B: It is only symbolic poetry and has no theological meaning.
choices.C: It teaches three separate gods acting independently.
choices.D: It shows the Spirit replaces the Son.

teaching.title: Triadic pattern in the earliest Gospel layer
teaching.body: The baptism scenes place the Father (voice), the Son (Jesus), and the Spirit (descending) together. That doesn’t read like a later add-on; it’s embedded in the narrative fabric of the Synoptics. Apologetically, it supports the claim that the NT contains the raw data of Trinitarian revelation before later councils formalized the language.
